owasp_top10: A08:2021 - Software and Data Integrity Failures
mitre_attack: T1059.007 - Command and Scripting Interpreter: JavaScript
cwe: CWE-915 - Improperly Controlled Modification of Dynamically-Determined Object Attributes

threat.title: Insecure Java Deserialization Leading to Remote Code Execution in Spring Boot
threat.description: Java deserialization vulnerabilities allow attackers to execute arbitrary code by sending maliciously crafted serialized objects to vulnerable endpoints. When applications deserialize untrusted data without proper validation, attackers can leverage gadget chains in libraries like Apache Commons Collections to achieve remote code execution (RCE), complete system compromise, and data exfiltration. This vulnerability class has been responsible for some of the most severe data breaches in history, including the 2017 Equifax breach.

attack.scenario:
1. Attacker identifies a Spring Boot API endpoint that accepts serialized Java objects (e.g., REST endpoint with Content-Type: application/x-java-serialized-object)
2. Attacker discovers vulnerable dependencies like Apache Commons Collections or outdated Spring Kafka versions in the application
3. Attacker crafts a malicious serialized payload using tools like ysoserial, creating a gadget chain that executes system commands
4. Attacker sends the weaponized payload to the vulnerable endpoint via HTTP POST request
5. The application deserializes the untrusted object, triggering the gadget chain and executing arbitrary code with application privileges
6. Attacker establishes a reverse shell, escalates privileges, and exfiltrates sensitive data from the database and file system
prerequisites: Application uses Java ObjectInputStream or similar deserialization mechanisms
Vulnerable dependencies present (e.g., Commons Collections < 3.2.2, Spring Kafka < 3.0.10)
Application deserializes data from untrusted sources without validation
Gadget classes available in the application's classpath for exploitation

cve_references.description: Apache Commons Collections deserialization vulnerability allowing remote code execution via InvokerTransformer
Apache Struts2 remote code execution via Content-Type header exploitation (root cause of Equifax breach)
Spring for Apache Kafka deserialization vulnerability when improperly configured with ErrorHandlingDeserializer

real_world_breaches.impact: 147 million individuals affected; exposed SSNs, birth dates, addresses, and credit card numbers; $1.38 billion total cost including $575 million settlement
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Failure to patch Apache Struts CVE-2017-5638 deserialization vulnerability despite patch being available 2 months prior

real_world_breaches.impact: Thousands of enterprise applications compromised within 48 hours of vulnerability disclosure; widespread RCE attacks
real_world_breaches.root_cause: CVE-2017-5638 exploitation in the wild before organizations could apply patches

bug_bounty_reports.summary: Java deserialization RCE via Spring Boot actuator endpoint accepting serialized objects

security_research.title: An In-depth Study of Java Deserialization Remote-Code Execution Exploits and Vulnerabilities
authors: ACM Transactions on Software Engineering and Methodology
security_research.key_finding: Comprehensive analysis of 249 CVEs related to Java deserialization, identifying systematic patterns in gadget chain construction

security_research.title: Java Deserialization Cheat Sheet
security_research.key_finding: Documented over 50 vulnerable Java libraries and frameworks susceptible to deserialization attacks

mitigations.title: Use Safe Serialization with JSON Instead of Java Serialization
mitigations.description: Replace Java's native ObjectInputStream serialization with JSON-based serialization using Jackson or Gson. JSON serialization is inherently safer as it doesn't allow arbitrary code execution during deserialization and provides better control over object instantiation.
nist_csf: PR.DS-5 - Protections against data leaks are implemented
PR.IP-2 - A System Development Life Cycle is implemented
validation_steps: Remove all ObjectInputStream usage from codebase
Configure Jackson as default serializer in Spring Boot
Enable strict JSON schema validation with @Valid annotations
Test endpoints with malicious payloads using Burp Suite
Verify no java.io.Serializable classes are exposed in APIs

mitigations.title: Implement ObjectInputStream Filtering with Java Deserialization Filters
mitigations.description: If Java serialization is absolutely required, use Java 9+ deserialization filters (JEP 290) to create allowlists of permitted classes and reject all other deserialization attempts. This prevents gadget chain exploitation by blocking dangerous classes.
nist_csf: PR.PT-3 - The principle of least functionality is incorporated
validation_steps: Implement ObjectInputFilter on all ObjectInputStream instances
Test with ysoserial payloads to ensure gadget chains are blocked
Monitor logs for rejected deserialization attempts
Verify JVM-wide filter is active via -Djdk.serialFilter
Conduct penetration testing with known Java deserialization exploits

mitigations.title: Upgrade Dependencies and Enable Dependency Scanning
mitigations.description: Keep all Spring Boot dependencies up-to-date, especially Spring Kafka, Spring AMQP, and Apache Commons. Use OWASP Dependency-Check and Snyk to continuously scan for known deserialization vulnerabilities in transitive dependencies.

code_example.code:
<!-- Spring Boot 3.2.x with secure dependencies -->
<project>
    <parent>
        <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
        <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-parent</artifactId>
        <version>3.2.2</version> <!-- Latest stable version -->
    </parent>
    
    <dependencies>
        <!-- Spring Boot Core -->
        <dependency>
            <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
            <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-web</artifactId>
        </dependency>
        
        <!-- PATCHED: Spring Kafka (CVE-2023-34040 fixed) -->
        <dependency>
            <groupId>org.springframework.kafka</groupId>
            <artifactId>spring-kafka</artifactId>
            <version>3.1.1</version> <!-- Minimum: 3.0.10 for Boot 3.x -->
        </dependency>
        
        <!-- PATCHED: Spring AMQP (CVE-2023-34050 fixed) -->
        <dependency>
            <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
            <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-amqp</artifactId>
            <!-- Inherits 3.1.0+ from parent, which includes fix -->
        </dependency>
        
        <!-- SAFE: Use latest Jackson (no deserialization vulns) -->
        <dependency>
            <groupId>com.fasterxml.jackson.core</groupId>
            <artifactId>jackson-databind</artifactId>
            <version>2.16.1</version>
        </dependency>
    </dependencies>
    
    <!-- OWASP Dependency-Check Plugin -->
    <build>
        <plugins>
            <plugin>
                <groupId>org.owasp</groupId>
                <artifactId>dependency-check-maven</artifactId>
                <version>9.0.9</version>
                <configuration>
                    <failBuildOnCVSS>7</failBuildOnCVSS> <!-- Fail on High/Critical -->
                    <suppressionFile>dependency-check-suppressions.xml</suppressionFile>
                </configuration>
                <executions>
                    <execution>
                        <goals>
                            <goal>check</goal>
                        </goals>
                    </execution>
                </executions>
            </plugin>
            
            <!-- Snyk Maven Plugin -->
            <plugin>
                <groupId>io.snyk</groupId>
                <artifactId>snyk-maven-plugin</artifactId>
                <version>2.2.0</version>
                <executions>
                    <execution>
                        <id>snyk-test</id>
                        <goals>
                            <goal>test</goal>
                        </goals>
                    </execution>
                </executions>
            </plugin>
        </plugins>
    </build>
</project>

iso27001_controls: A.14.2.4 - Restrictions on changes to software packages
nist_csf: PR.IP-12 - A vulnerability management plan is developed and implemented
validation_steps: Run mvn dependency-check:check and review report
Configure Dependabot alerts in GitHub repository
Set up Snyk monitoring for continuous vulnerability tracking
Establish SLA for patching Critical/High CVEs (e.g., 48 hours)
Verify no Commons Collections < 3.2.2 in dependency tree

indicators: Unexpected ObjectInputStream errors or InvalidClassException in logs
Base64-encoded Java serialization magic bytes (rO0AB) in request payloads
Sudden spike in JVM process CPU usage or OutOfMemoryError exceptions
Network connections to external IPs from Java processes (reverse shell indicators)
Deserialization of classes from org.apache.commons.collections.functors package
Abnormal command execution logs (Runtime.exec, ProcessBuilder) during request processing

log_queries.query:
// Detect Java deserialization attacks via suspicious Content-Type headers
AppServiceHTTPLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(1h)
| where ContentType contains "java-serialized" or ContentType contains "application/x-java"
| extend PayloadPreview = substring(RequestBody, 0, 20)
| where PayloadPreview startswith "rO0AB"  // Java serialization magic bytes (Base64)
| project TimeGenerated, ClientIp, Uri, Method, StatusCode, ContentType, UserAgent
| summarize AttackCount = count() by ClientIp, bin(TimeGenerated, 5m)
| where AttackCount > 3
| order by AttackCount desc

index=springboot sourcetype=spring:application:log
| search ("ObjectInputStream" OR "readObject" OR "InvokerTransformer" OR "InvalidClassException")
| rex field=_raw "(?<blocked_class>org\\.apache\\.commons\\.collections\\.functors\\.\\w+)"
| stats count by blocked_class, host, _time
| where count > 5
| eval severity="CRITICAL"
| table _time, host, blocked_class, count, severity

event.category: "web" AND (
  http.request.headers.content-type: "*java-serialized*" OR
  http.request.body.content: *rO0AB* OR
  process.command_line: (*ysoserial* OR *ObjectInputStream*)
)
AND http.response.status_code: (200 OR 500)
| stats count by source.ip, url.path, process.name

tools.name: Burp Suite Java Deserialization Scanner
tools.usage: Scan endpoints for Java serialization acceptance and test with gadget chains

tools.name: Contrast Security Assess
tools.usage: Real-time detection of unsafe deserialization at runtime
